coredata.dc:title: Multiple immobilized borate composites achieving Color-Tunable long afterglow in aqueous media for bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Tao
Hao, Chenxia
Bai, Yunfeng
Chen, Pengli
Ma, Tao
Hong, Guifang
Yin, Chenguang
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: The afterglow phenomenon has garnered significant attention due to its exceptional luminescent properties, yet achieving long-lived and color-tunable afterglow in aqueous media remains a challenging task. In this article, we present a facile hydrothermal strategy for the preparation of borate afterglow materials (APB@CC) using ammonium pentaborate and cyanuric chloride as starting materials. The resultant APB@CC displays color-tunable long afterglows from blue to green in aqueous media. Further studies show that the synergistic effect of covalent and hydrogen bonds effectively promotes the immobilization and rigidification of the triplet state, enabling afterglow emission in aqueous media. Encouraged by color-tunability in solutions, we implemented their potential applications in bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting. This work not only provides insights for extending borate afterglow materials in aqueous media, but also will facilitate the development of high signal-to-noise bioimaging and advanced anti-counterfeiting techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Afterglow
Color-tunable
Aqueous media
Imaging
Anti-counterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Powder XRD patterns of ammonium pentaborate (pAPB), cyanuric chloride (CC), and APB@CC. (b) Raman spectrum of APB@CC. (c) FTIR spectra of pAPB, CC, and APB@CC. (d) XPS survey spectrum of APB@CC. High-resolution XPS (e) N ls and (f) C ls spectra of APB@CC.

(a) Afterglow excitation-emission mapping of APB@CC solution under ambient conditions, the upper inset shows the afterglow spectra excited at 295 nm and 485 nm. (b) Afterglow emission color trajectory of APB@CC solution recorded at excitation wavelengths from 295 nm to 485 nm in the CIE coordinate diagram. (c) Afterglow emission spectra of APB@CC solution at 405 nm, 410 nm and 415 nm excitation. (d) Afterglow decay curves of APB@CC solution recorded at 385 nm and 530 nm.
Confocal images of APB@CC at 405 nm and 488 nm laser excitation of MCF-7 cells, respectively.
Multi-response security protection application for APB@CC.
Illustration for the preparation process and afterglow photographs after excitation at 310 and 460 nm of APB@CC aqueous solution.

head.ce:title: Multiple immobilized borate composites achieving Color-Tunable long afterglow in aqueous media for bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Engineering Research Center of Coal-Based Ecological Carbon Sequestration Technology of the Ministry of Education, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Engineering Research Center of Coal-Based Ecological Carbon Sequestration Technology of the Ministry of Education
Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Engineering Research Center of Coal-Based Ecological Carbon Sequestration Technology of the Ministry of Education, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The afterglow phenomenon has garnered significant attention due to its exceptional luminescent properties, yet achieving long-lived and color-tunable afterglow in aqueous media remains a challenging task. In this article, we present a facile hydrothermal strategy for the preparation of borate afterglow materials (APB@CC) using ammonium pentaborate and cyanuric chloride as starting materials. The resultant APB@CC displays color-tunable long afterglows from blue to green in aqueous media. Further studies show that the synergistic effect of covalent and hydrogen bonds effectively promotes the immobilization and rigidification of the triplet state, enabling afterglow emission in aqueous media. Encouraged by color-tunability in solutions, we implemented their potential applications in bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting. This work not only provides insights for extending borate afterglow materials in aqueous media, but also will facilitate the development of high signal-to-noise bioimaging and advanced anti-counterfeiting techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Optical technologies, renowned for their high sensitivity, non-invasiveness, and high spatio-temporal resolution, have been applied in the fields of bioimaging, sensing, and anti-counterfeiting [1–5]. Among these technologies, afterglow emission with a long lifetime, usually known as phosphorescence or delayed fluorescence, has become a current research hotspot due to its emission properties related to triplet excitons, thus providing a promising platform for a variety of advanced applications, such as time-resolved imaging, multilevel anti-counterfeiting, and so on [6–9]. Despite the attractive prospects and progress made, it is still quite challenging to achieve long-lived afterglow emission due to the fact that triplet excitons are easily deactivated during non-radiative decay processes.

Afterglow materials, which can continue to emit for seconds or even hours after the excitation light is turned off [10–12]. Afterglow materials currently mainly include inorganic metal materials and organic molecule materials [13–15]. The afterglow emission of inorganic metal materials originates from on the traps formed by structure defects or complex impurities, in which harsh high-temperature sintering process, high cost and cytotoxicity are the main hindrances to their practical applications [16,17]. To obtain organic molecule materials with long afterglow, a rigid environment needs to be constructed by host–guest doping [18,19] H-type aggregation [20–22], crystal engineering [23,24] or many others [25–27], which leads to the fact that the afterglow emission is most likely to occur in crystals or films, thus limiting its application. Achieving afterglow emission in solution, especially in aqueous solution, remains a formidable challenge. Moreover, these afterglow materials are mainly based on different carbon skeletons and stacking motifs, and most of them show only single-wavelength emission. Therefore, there is an urgent need to construct new, nontoxic and color-tunable afterglow materials in aqueous solution for practical applications.

Although boron-based materials, analogous to carbon dots, have been intensively investigated as a new class of afterglow materials, the research of boron-containing material in this field is rather limited. Just one report of Lian and his co-workers developed a boron-based material with long afterglow in solution by embedding the luminescent center (boron nitride dots) in an isogenous B2O3 matrix, which effectively protects the luminescent centers from being quenched by water or oxygen, thus enabling the material with afterglow emission [28]. Further, experimental and theoretical calculations have proposed that boron-oxygen units can be used as efficient luminescent centres [29,30]. This knowledge prompted us to design boron-oxygen luminescent centers in matrices that would endow the materials with afterglow features.

Herein, we developed a facile hydrothermal strategy for the preparation of borate afterglow materials (APB@CC) by immobilizing the luminescent centers of ammonium pentaborate into cyanuric chloride matrix. Interestingly, the aqueous solution of APB@CC displays color-tunable afterglows from blue to green after irradiation by excitation light sources of different wavelengths. Further studies show that the synergistic effect of covalent and hydrogen bonds plays a critical role in activating the afterglow observed in aqueous media. Based on these unique properties in aqueous media, APB@CC has been successfully applied to high signal-to-noise bioimaging and multilevel anti-counterfeiting.
Ammonium pentaborate and cyanuric chloride were purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd, and used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) was used to characterize the morphology. Dynamic light scattering analyzer (DLS, DelsaMax PRO) was used to measure the particle size distribution. Powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku SmartLab SE) was used to collect crystallography data. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) was used to collect spectroscopy data. Laser scanning confocal microscope was used for bioimaging. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Nicolet iS20) and Raman spectrometer (Raman, Renishaw inVia) were used to measure FTIR and Raman spectra, respectively. HORIBA QM 8000 spectrofluorometer was used to measure photoluminescence spectra, absolute quantum yields, and decay curves at room temperature. Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrofluorometer was used to measure photoluminescence spectra at low temperatures and temperature-dependent afterglow emission spectra.

Ammonium pentaborate (0.5 g) and cyanuric chloride (0.01 g) were dissolved in ultrapure water (20 mL) and stirred continuously for 30 min. Then, the solution was transferred to a poly(p-phenylene) reactor and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature. The resulting solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 5 h to obtain the purified powder for characterization.
APB was obtained by the same procedure as APB@CC, only without the addition of cyanuric chloride.

The cytotoxicity of APB@CC was evaluated in human breast cancer (MCF-7) cells using the standard MTT assay. MCF-7 cells were first inoculated into 96-well plates at a density of 5000 cells per well and incubated for 12 h at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. Subsequently, different concentrations of APB@CC solution (0, 25, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg/mL) were introduced into each well and incubated for 24 h. After incubation, MTT solution (10 μL, 5 mg/mL) was pipetted into each well and co-incubated with the cells for 4 h. Finally, the absorbance was measured at 570 nm after discarding the supernatant and adding 150 μL of DMSO to each well.

For cell imaging, MCF-7 cells were inoculated into confocal dishes at a cell density of 1.5 × 105 per mL and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. Then APB@CC solution (10 μL, 100 μg/mL) was added and co-incubated with the cells for 12 h. The supernatant was discarded and the cells were washed 3 times with PBS, and images were acquired under a laser scanning confocal microscope.

Borate long afterglow materials were prepared through a simple one-step hydrothermal process using ammonium pentaborate and cyanuric chloride as starting materials. The resultant composites (APB@CC) were well dispersed in water as a colloid, without precipitation for months. The aqueous solution of APB@CC showed a long-lived afterglow with tunable color, indicating that the formation of the rigid framework effectively prevented the quenching by external factors (e.g., oxygen molecules and water molecules). The schematic of the preparation process is presented in Scheme 1 .

The APB@CC aqueous solution exhibited a typical Tyndall effect (Fig. S1). DLS result showed that the average hydrodynamic diameter of APB@CC in water was around 254 nm (Fig. S2). TEM and XRD characterized the morphology and phase structure of APB@CC. As shown in Fig. S3 TEM image, APB@CC has an irregular morphology with a size of about 200 nm. Similar XRD patterns were observed from APB@CC and ammonium pentaborate (Fig. 1 a), and both were consistent with the standard card of NH4B5O8·4H2O (PDF#31–0043), indicating that ammonium pentaborate did not undergo condensation to form B2O3 during the hydrothermal process [31]. Therefore, APB@CC is considered to be a borate composite material. However, it can be seen that the addition of a small amount of cyanuric chloride resulted in a significant change in the intensity of X-ray diffraction peaks of APB@CC at 31.6° (040) of APB@CC compared to ammonium pentaborate, suggesting that the low doping of cyanuric chloride slightly affects the stacking of ammonium pentaborate molecules.

Raman and FTIR data also provides the evidence for the borate materials nature of APB@CC. The Raman peaks at 466 cm−1 and 519 cm−1 were matched with O-B-O ring symmetric bending vibration, the strong peak appearing at 564 cm−1 was ascribed to O-B-O terminal bending vibration absorption, and the peaks at 776 cm−1, 793 cm−1 and 926 cm−1 belong to B-O ring stretching vibration (Fig. 1b) [32]. The main FTIR absorption peaks of ammonium pentaborate can be observed in the spectrum of APB@CC (Fig. 1c). Among them, the O-H and N-H stretching vibrations band appear from 3000 to 3500 cm−1 [33]. The bands located at 1653 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 were related to NH4 asymmetric bending and symmetric bending vibrations [34]. The B-O vibrations were observed at 780–1456 cm−1 [32,34]. The characteristic peaks at 691 cm−1 and 594 cm−1 were assigned to O-B-O ring asymmetric and terminal bending vibrations, respectively [32,34]. In comparison, a new peak corresponding to the C = N stretching vibration from cyanuric chloride appeared at 1730 cm−1 [35], demonstrating that chemical reactions might have taken place during the hydrothermal process.

To further confirm the chemical characteristics of APB@CC, XPS measurements were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1d, APB@CC was consisted of the elements B, C, N and O with the contents of 35.04 %, 12.52 %, 5.16 %, and 47.28 %. The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s and B 1 s (Fig. S4) were mainly fitted to B-O. The XPS N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1e) displayed two peaks at 400.8 eV and 398.7 eV for the N = C-N and N-H bonds, where the N-H bond is derived from NH4 + [36]. The XPS C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1f) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 288.2, 285.5 and 283.7 eV for the C-O-B, C-N and C-C bonds [37]. The presence of C-O-B bonds confirms that ammonium pentaborate and cyanuric chloride are bonded via covalent bond (as illustrated in Scheme 1).

Subsequently, the photophysical properties of APB@CC solution were investigated in detail. Fig. S5 shows the fluorescence excitation-emission spectra of APB@CC solution. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence exhibits a clear bathochromic shift as excitation wavelength increases from 310 nm to 500 nm, with the main peak changing from 390 nm to 530 nm. Fig. S6 shows the fluorescence decay curves of APB@CC solution fitted by a biexponential function. The emission band lifetime at 390 nm is 5.14 ns with components of 1.59 ns (93.23 %) and 11.76 ns (6.77 %); the lifetime at 530 nm is 5.47 ns with components of 24.87 ns (1.26 %) and 3.90 ns (98.74 %) (Table S1). The fluorescence quantum yields of APB@CC solution excited at 310 nm and 500 nm are 5.0 % and 4.5 %, respectively.

The afterglow excitation-emission spectra of APB@CC solution are shown in Fig. 2 a. When the excitation wavelength increases from 295 nm to 485 nm, the main emission peak varies from 385 nm to 530 nm, exhibiting excitation-dependent photoluminescence property similar to fluorescence. The color variations of APB@CC solution in response to different excitation wavelengths are shown in the CIE coordinate diagram in Fig. 2b. When the excitation wavelength is less than 410 nm, blue light is stronger than green light; and when the excitation wavelength is greater than 410 nm, the photoluminescence is dominated by green light (Fig. 2c). The color of the afterglow photographs of APB@CC solution after excitation at 310 and 460 nm changes from blue to green, which is good linearity of CIE coordinates (Scheme 1). Fig. 2d shows the afterglow decay curves of APB@CC solution fitted by a biexponential function. The lifetime of the emission band at 385 nm is 5.50 ms with lifetime components of 5.49 ms (50.98 %) and 5.53 ms (49.02 %), and the lifetime at 530 nm is 6.17 ms with lifetime components of 6.20 ms (48.90 %) and 6.15 ms (51.10 %) (Table S2). Excitation-dependent and multi-exponential decays indicate the presence of different decay channels. The afterglow quantum yields of APB@CC solution excited at 295 nm and 485 nm are 3.9 % and 3.6 %, respectively.

In order to explore the source of the afterglow emission in APB@CC solution, the afterglow emission spectra with temperature changes were measured (Fig. S7, S8). As shown in Fig. S7, two emission peaks are observed with temperature variation, possibly attributed to changes in the electronic energy level structure of the sample at different temperatures [38]. At 80 K, the afterglow spectrum excited at 295 nm reveals a main emission peak at 450 nm, which gradually decreases in intensity from 80 K to 200 K. This is typical characteristic of phosphorescence. The reason is because the low temperature can effectively limit the vibration and largely suppress the non-radiative transition, thus enhancing the phosphorescence emission. The afterglow lifetime of 450 nm at 80 K is calculated to be 1.43 s (Fig. S9, Table S3). The intensity of the emission peak at 385 nm decreases. This may be due to the suppression of the reverse intersystem crossing process of delayed fluorescence at lower temperatures, with phosphorescence predominating [39]. As the temperature rises from 200 K to 280 K, the intensity of the peak at 385 nm increases significantly. This could be attributed to the activation of delayed fluorescence within this temperature range, resulting in an increase in emission peak intensity [40]. Therefore, as the temperature continues to increase from 80 K, the phosphorescence intensity decreases and delayed fluorescence intensity increases, leading to the gradual conversion of phosphorescence to delayed fluorescence. The same phenomenon is also observed for the intensity of the afterglow excited at 485 nm when the temperature is higher than 240 K. In addition, the energy gap (ΔE ST) between the lowest singlet excited state (S1) and the lowest triplet excited state (T1) is calculated to be less than 0.2 eV (Table S4), which is sufficient to facilitate the reverse intersystem crossing [41,42]. The fluorescence and afterglow emission spectra of APB@CC solution at 77 K are given in Fig. S10.

To confirm the photoluminescence mechanism, as a control experiment, APB was prepared by the same hydrothermal method as that of APB@CC, with only ammonium pentaborate (pAPB) as raw material and no addition of cyanuric chloride (CC). It should be pointed out pAPB has no afterglow. Controlled experiments revealed that APB in solid state exhibited weak afterglow emission similar to that of APB@CC, whereas no afterglow was observed for APB in aqueous media (Fig. S10a). This phenomenon infers that APB@CC and APB may have the homologous photoluminescence mechanism, in addition to the fact that CC has a positive effect on the afterglow emission of APB@CC in aqueous media.

The chemical composition of APB was investigated to gain insight into its underlying mechanism. There was no significant difference in the XRD pattern (Fig. S11a) and Raman spectrum (Fig. S11b) of APB compared to APB@CC, indicating that the composition of APB is still dominated by the molecular structure unit of ammonium pentaborate. Afterglow emission may derive from B-O alternating six-membered ring structure with characteristic n-π* and π-π* transitions; it may also give rise to multiple energy levels, forming a double emission channel [30,43,44]. The FTIR spectrum of APB (Fig. S11c) shows O-H and N-H absorption bands at 3500–3000 cm−1, indicating the presence of hydrogen bonds. Hydrogen bonds, as a strongly directional non-covalent force, play a crucial role in suppressing the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons and improving afterglow performance, but often cannot completely isolate the luminescent material from quenching factors. High concentration of dissolved oxygen in water can induce non-radiative decay, while molecular motion can lead to the deactivation of triplet exciton states. The rest of the FTIR peaks are derived from B-O bonds and N-H bonds, which can also be seen from the XPS (Fig. S12). The results of the control experiment reveal that the covalent bonds and hydrogen bonds synergistically protect the excited triplet excitons from quenching, thus enabling the afterglow emission of APB@CC in aqueous media.

According to the above analysis, the photoluminescence mechanism of APB@CC composites is proposed as follows. During the hydrothermal process, CC is rapidly hydrolyzed to cyanuric acid [45], followed by condensation with pAPB to form C-O-B covalent bonds and hydrogen bonds. Hydrogen bonds indeed stabilize the triplet state to achieve afterglow, also as reported in previous literature [46,47], but not strongly enough to activate the afterglow of APB in aqueous media. To compensate, the formation of covalent bonds effectively drives immobilization and rigidification the triplet state, preventing the quenching by water molecules, and thus this multiple fixation-assisted effects achieves afterglow emission in aqueous media.

Benefiting from the unique color-tunable long afterglow performance of APB@CC in aqueous media, we further explored the potential application in bioimaging. The cytotoxicity of APB@CC was firstly assessed in MCF-7 cells using the standard MTT assay. The results showed that APB@CC was cultured with MCF-7 cells at concentrations up to 200 μg/mL for 24 h, and the cell viability remained above 85 %, indicating that APB@CC has low cytotoxicity to MCF-7 cells and can be used for cell imaging. Subsequently, MCF-7 cells were cultured in the presence of APB@CC (100 μg/mL) for 12 h. As can be seen from the bright-field and fluorescence images (Fig. 3 ), MCF-7 cells showed blue and green fluorescence under excitation at 405 nm and 488 nm, respectively, indicating that APB@CC had good cell permeability. More importantly, after turning off the 405 nm and 488 nm excitation light sources, the afterglow images in MCF-7 cells could be clearly captured with blue and green afterglows, respectively. Due to the complementary nature of multi-channel afterglow signals in the probing process, multi-emission afterglow materials can significantly improve the probing capability and show great potential in bioimaging.

In addition, the anti-counterfeiting application of the material was also preliminary investigated. APB and BNDs@B2O3 [28] materials were introduced for comparison, wherein the aqueous solutions of APB@CC, APB and BNDs@B2O3 can be directly used as inks. As shown in Fig. 4 , the flower patterns are not visible under sunlight. According to the results of the previous analysis, APB@CC and APB exhibited dynamic photostimulation-responsive afterglow luminescence. After 310 nm excitation, they showed blue patterns; while after 460 nm excitation, they showed green patterns. In addition, APB@CC exhibits good water-stimulation response performance, emitting clear blue afterglow emission after water vapor wetting followed by 310 nm excitation, and a green pattern after 460 nm excitation. In contrast, APB may lose the afterglow emission due to intermolecular hydrogen bonds under the influence of water vapor wetting. On the other hand, BNDs@B2O3 exhibits static afterglow properties, showing monochromatic afterglow characteristics. After 310 nm excitation, it shows obvious blue afterglow emission; after humid treatment, it also shows blue afterglow after 310 nm excitation again. Based on the above phenomena, APB@CC shows higher concealment and security in the anti-counterfeiting field.

In summary, we have prepared APB@CC composites using a facile hydrothermal strategy with ammonium pentaborate and cyanuric chloride as starting materials. APB@CC displays color-tunable long afterglows from blue to green in aqueous media. Further studies revealed that the formation of covalent bonds between ammonium pentaborate and cyanuric chloride promote the immobilization and rigidification of the triplet state, thus activating the multi-mode afterglow in aqueous media. Besides, hydrogen bonds between ammonium pentaborate and cyanuric chloride will also contribute to better the immobilization of the triplet state. Furthermore, we explore their potential applications in bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting. This work not only provides insights for extending borate-based afterglow materials in aqueous media, but also will facilitate the development of high signal-to-noise bioimaging and multilevel anti-counterfeiting.

Tao Zhou: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Chenxia Hao: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Yunfeng Bai: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Pengli Chen: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Tao Ma: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Guifang Hong: Investigation, Formal analysis. Chenguang Yin: Investigation, Formal analysis. Feng Feng: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2022L427, 2023L271), Science Research Foundation of Shanxi Datong University (2022Q13), and Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (202303021211324, 202303021222212).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110653.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
